metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'moderately large text' in AC#4 with a specific size bound, e.g. 'a string of at least 1 000 characters (≈1 KB)'.
findings.finding: AC#4 uses 'moderately large text' as a corpus entry size, which is subjective and not measurable — an executor cannot determine whether their chosen size satisfies the criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: 'a string of at least 1 000 Unicode characters (e.g. 1 000 repetitions of a Latin letter).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly document whether the pre-existing `→`→`->` normalization is in scope for this ticket (exempt it from byte-identity guarantees, or remove the normalization, or note it in the policy decisions) so round-trip test assertions can be written correctly.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 and corpus AC require byte-identical round-trips ('stored as-is', 'no silent trimming', 'round-trip assertions expect the original bytes back'), but the existing `create_core` (line 121) and `edit_core` (line 385) both apply `title.replace('→', '->')` before storing. Titles containing U+2192 '→' will NOT round-trip byte-identically. The plan never acknowledges this pre-existing normalization or exempts it from the byte-identity guarantee, so round-trip test assertions will fail for that character.
findings.location: What section (policy: 'stored as-is') / AC line 3 ('stored byte-identically') / src/rebar/_commands/composer.py lines 121, 385
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the 'What' policy section: 'The pre-existing U+2192 → `->` normalization is preserved (not a silent mutation introduced by this ticket); round-trip assertions must account for it, or a separate ticket must remove it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'byte-identical round-trip' oracle in AC4 and the 'stored as-is' language in the 'What' section with the pre-existing `title.replace('→', '->')` mutation (composer.py:121, edit_core:385): either carve out U+2192 from the corpus explicitly ('corpus excludes characters with documented pre-existing normalization'), or restate the oracle as 'byte-identical modulo the documented → substitution' so implementers write correct assertions.
findings.finding: The plan's 'byte-identical round-trip' test oracle in AC4 is undermined by the pre-existing title.replace('→','->') mutation (composer.py:121, edit_core:385) that this plan does NOT change. The corpus description does not exclude U+2192, so an implementer who includes it will get a spurious assertion failure, or — if they avoid it by accident — will produce a corpus that silently misses the only existing documented non-round-trip mutation. The oracle is ambiguous and will produce incorrect tests unless the plan reconciles 'stored as-is / byte-identical' with the existing arrow normalization.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to AC4: 'The corpus excludes U+2192 (→), which has a pre-existing documented substitution to "->" and is intentionally NOT round-tripped byte-identically; that substitution is tested separately.' And update the 'What' section's 'stored as-is' language to read 'stored as-is for all characters except the pre-existing → normalization'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'byte-identical round-trip / do NOT normalize' policy with the existing `title.replace('→', '->')` substitution applied in both `create_core` and `edit_core`: either (a) document '→' as a documented exception to byte-identity and exclude it from corpus round-trip assertions, or (b) remove the arrow substitution as part of this plan and update its docs, or (c) reframe the policy as 'no new normalization' rather than absolute byte-identity.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'byte-identical round-trip; do NOT normalize' for all corpus values in AC#3 and AC#4, but the existing code already performs a character substitution (`title.replace('→', '->')`) on every stored title in both `create_core` (line 121) and `edit_core` (line 385). Any corpus value containing U+2192 (→) will be mutated before storage, falsifying the byte-identity oracle for that entry. The plan does not acknowledge this pre-existing normalization, leaving the test corpus specification contradictory with the codebase behavior.
findings.location: What section ('Unicode normalization' policy) / AC#3 / AC#4; `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` lines 121 and 385
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clarifying sentence to the 'Unicode normalization' policy block: 'Exception: the existing U+2192 (→) → "->" substitution is retained (not removed by this plan); corpus entries containing → must assert the stored form "->" rather than the original → character, or exclude → from the byte-identity corpus.'

findings.finding: AC3 asserts that corpus values are 'rejected identically across all three facades' but never names or defines the shared rejection identity schema (exit code, exception type, error message pattern). The codebase has at least three distinct rejection surfaces — CLI exit code + stderr, library RebarError, MCP ToolError — and the plan does not specify which attributes must match for rejection to be 'identical'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'or is rejected identically across all three facades'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add concrete proving commands for AC#6 and AC#7: e.g., for AC#6 a named script/query command and its expected output; for AC#7 a `grep`-based check that CHANGELOG and help text contain the required wording.
findings.finding: AC#6 (store scan) and AC#7 (docs + CHANGELOG update) each lack a proving command — `make lint && make typecheck && make test` does not exercise them, leaving no way to verify these criteria are met.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC#6 and AC#7; Scope section (no step for docs or store scan)
findings.suggested_fix: AC#6: 'Run `python -c "from rebar.store import scan_titles; print(scan_titles())"` (or equivalent) and assert output is empty.' AC#7: 'Assert `grep -q "whitespace" CHANGELOG.md` and `rebar --help | grep -q "whitespace"`.'

findings.finding: AC5 (store scan) and AC6 (docs/CHANGELOG updated) have no associated proving command — there is no specified grep, diff, or review step to produce evidence that either the scan was run or that the docs/CHANGELOG were actually modified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC list: 'Verified by: `grep -i "whitespace" CHANGELOG.md | grep -q .` and `grep -i "whitespace" docs/cli-reference.md | grep -q .` (both exit 0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC#6, replace the open-ended 'confirm whether' investigation with a committed decision: state either (a) 'the scan must find zero whitespace-only titles or the ticket will add a migration step before enabling the validation' or (b) 'whitespace-only stored titles are ignored by the new validation and no migration is required' — so the executor has no branch to invent.
findings.finding: AC#6 contains a 'confirm whether any stored tickets have whitespace-only titles' investigation step with no specified action branch — if whitespace-only titles ARE found, the plan does not say what to do (migrate, abort, skip), deferring a real design decision to the executor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC#6: 'confirm whether any stored tickets have whitespace-only titles (a one-off store scan) so no migration is silently required'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'If the scan finds any whitespace-only titles, open a follow-on migration ticket and do NOT merge this change until that ticket is resolved. If zero are found, document the count in the PR description and proceed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the pre-existing `→`→`->` title substitution (composer.py lines 121 and 385) interacts with the byte-identity corpus oracle: either (a) exclude U+2192 from the corpus explicitly, (b) document that the substitution is intentional and exempt from byte-identity, or (c) state that the substitution is being removed as part of this change with a corresponding AC and CHANGELOG entry.
findings.finding: The plan's byte-identity corpus oracle conflicts with the pre-existing `→`→`->` character substitution in composer.py (create_core line 121, edit_core line 385), both of which are in the plan's scope. The plan's 'Unicode normalization: preserve as-is (byte-identical round-trip); do NOT normalize' rule does not carve out this existing substitution, leaving the oracle undefined for any corpus entry containing U+2192 (→). An implementer cannot write an unambiguous round-trip assertion for such a title.
findings.location: Plan: 'What' section (Unicode normalization policy) + AC bullet 4 ('round-trips byte-identically'); Code: composer.py lines 121 and 385
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'Committed policy decisions' section: 'The pre-existing U+2192 (→) → "->" substitution is NOT removed by this change; corpus entries containing → are excluded from round-trip assertions (or are listed with their expected stored form after substitution).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add deliverables for (a) a one-off store scan / migration check and back-out note, and (b) docs + CHANGELOG update to the 'What' section, and add corresponding file paths (e.g. `CHANGELOG.md`, `docs/`) to the Scope so every AC has a described owner.
findings.finding: AC line 6 (breaking-change handling: back-out/expand-contract note + one-off store scan) and AC line 7 (user-facing docs, CLI help, API reference, CHANGELOG) describe deliverables that have no corresponding described work in the 'What' section and no files listed in the Scope — both are orphan acceptance criteria.
findings.location: AC lines 6–7 vs. 'What' section and Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: In 'What', add a bullet: 'Run a one-off scan of the ticket store for whitespace-only titles; document findings and a back-out procedure in CHANGELOG.md or a migration note.' Add 'CHANGELOG.md' and relevant docs paths to Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'Document a back-out/expand-contract note' with an explicit rollback/back-out plan: specify revert steps (e.g., feature flag, prior-version rollback), define what action to take if the store scan finds existing whitespace-only titles (e.g., migration script, warning-only mode), and if a warning-then-hard-reject phase is warranted, sequence it explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges the breaking change (whitespace-only title rejection) and requires 'a back-out/expand-contract note,' but never specifies the actual rollback mechanism or expand-contract sequence. T4 requires not just acknowledgement but an explicit, actionable rollback/back-out plan. Additionally, if the store scan finds existing whitespace-only tickets, the plan says only 'confirm... so no migration is silently required' — it does not define what to do if migration IS required.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete subsection: (1) If store scan finds whitespace-only titles → run migration script `scripts/migrate_whitespace_titles.py` that trims or flags them before deploying validation; (2) If the change must be reverted, document the one-line revert commit or feature-flag name that re-enables the old `if not title:` check; (3) If a grace period is preferred, add a `--strict-titles` CLI flag to the create/edit commands and default it to off in this release, switching the default in the next release (expand-contract).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the two policy decisions (surrounding-whitespace accepted as-is; NFC/NFD treated as distinct byte-identical values) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or named design-doc section — not just inline plan prose — so the rationale is durable.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two non-obvious, permanently load-bearing policy decisions — (1) accepting and storing surrounding-whitespace titles byte-identically (no trimming) and (2) treating NFC and NFD Unicode forms as distinct values — but captures them only in inline prose in the 'What' section. No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry is specified, so future maintainers have no durable record of the rationale when the 'What' section is no longer in view.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0042-title-whitespace-and-unicode-policy.md`) covering: (a) why surrounding whitespace is preserved rather than trimmed, (b) why Unicode normalization is explicitly not applied, and (c) the byte-identity round-trip oracle. Reference it from CLAUDE.md or the relevant module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC 6 to specify the action if the store scan finds whitespace-only-titled tickets: either (a) run a one-time migration to substitute a sentinel/trimmed value with an audit log, or (b) explicitly document that such tickets will require manual remediation and add a warning on read, so the rollout plan is complete.
findings.finding: AC 6 requires a one-off store scan to detect existing tickets with whitespace-only titles, but the plan specifies no action path if violations are found. After this change, any such ticket becomes uneditable (its title now fails validation on every edit attempt), yet the plan says only 'confirm... so no migration is silently required' — leaving the blast radius unspecified if the scan reveals matches.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether the cross-facade text corpus (AC 4–5) warrants a separate ticket so the whitespace-title bug fix (AC 1–3) can ship without coupling to corpus design decisions — or explicitly justify keeping them together (e.g., the corpus is the regression guard for the fix).
findings.finding: The plan bundles two independently releasable outcomes: (A) a targeted bug-fix — whitespace-only title rejection across CLI/library/MCP with its direct tests and docs — and (B) a broader cross-facade text corpus covering leading dashes, quotes, backslashes, LF/CRLF, emoji, and NFC/NFD round-trips. (A) could ship and deliver value without (B), and (B) could be planned as a general parity test suite independent of the whitespace fix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_io/_provenance.py` / `import_ndjson.py` as an affected consumer and decide whether to strip-or-skip whitespace-only titles at import time (rather than hard-fail mid-batch).
findings.finding: The import path `_io/_provenance.py` (line 24: `'title': record.get('title') or ''`) passes titles from imported NDJSON records directly to `create_ticket`, which routes through `create_core`. A whitespace-only title from an external source would now be rejected by the tightened validation, silently breaking the import pipeline. This caller is outside the named Scope and is not enumerated as a consumer requiring attention.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/_io/_provenance.py line 24 / src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py line 229

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit remediation path for pre-existing whitespace-only-title records discovered by the store scan (e.g. rename them, quarantine with a placeholder, or gate the validation tightening on a confirmed-clean scan result), and assert the store STATE after any rollback — not just that the code reverts.
findings.finding: AC line 6 mandates a one-off store scan for whitespace-only titles but specifies no remediation path if affected records are found. If such records exist in the store at the time of cutover, the new validation layer will reject them on any edit attempt, making them unreachable/uneditable with no defined back-out path for the persisted data — only the code can be rolled back, but the stored records remain stranded.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 6: 'confirm whether any stored tickets have whitespace-only titles (a one-off store scan) so no migration is silently required'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a gating step: if the store scan finds any whitespace-only-title records, block the deploy and require an explicit migration (e.g. rename each to a sentinel like '[untitled]' with operator confirmation, or provide a `--skip-whitespace-title-check` bypass) before enforcing the new validation. Document this gating condition in the AC and the CHANGELOG entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the Scope to list every source file (library entrypoint(s), MCP handler(s)) that must be modified to enforce whitespace-title rejection, matching the multi-facade requirement in 'What' and AC line 1.
findings.finding: The Scope section names only `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` for validation changes, but the 'What' section and AC line 1 require whitespace-only title rejection across CLI, library, AND MCP create/edit surfaces — implying additional source files need edits that are absent from the Scope.
findings.location: Scope section vs. 'What' section and AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: `src/rebar/_lib/<title-validation-module>.py` (or whichever module the library facade calls) and `src/rebar/_mcp/<handler>.py`, annotated with '(whitespace validation)'.

findings.finding: The plan says 'trim then check non-empty' but does not specify whether the trimmed value is also what gets stored (i.e., leading/trailing whitespace is silently stripped from the persisted title) or whether only the validation uses the stripped form and the original whitespace-padded string is stored. This distinction has a concrete observable consequence: AC3 mandates that the corpus 'round-trips identically', so if `' hello '` is passed and `'hello'` is stored, the identity assertion fails; if the original is stored, then `'   '` must be rejected but `' hello '` is accepted and preserved exactly. The plan's phrasing is ambiguous and the implementer must make a semantic call that affects both the code and the test corpus design.
findings.location: What section ('trim then check non-empty') and AC3 ('round-trips identically')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'What' or AC1 bullet: 'Whitespace is stripped before the non-empty check; the trimmed value is stored (so `"  hello  "` round-trips as `"hello"`).' This matches the analogous `→`→`->` normalisation already applied in create_core and makes the corpus design unambiguous.

findings.finding: AC3 references 'composed vs decomposed Unicode as separate preservation cases' in the corpus, implying a normalization policy decision rule, but that policy is never defined anywhere in the plan. The oracle for what 'round-trips identically' means when the same string appears as NFC in one facade and NFD in another is absent, leaving the test implementer with an undefined assertion target.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'composed vs decomposed Unicode as separate preservation cases'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Normalization policy: titles are stored byte-identical as received (no NFC/NFD normalization applied); the composed and decomposed corpus entries are therefore expected to round-trip to different byte sequences and must each be stored and replayed exactly as submitted.' OR 'The system normalizes to NFC at ingest; the decomposed form should round-trip as its NFC equivalent.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
